source_title: Essentials: Effects of Fasting & Time Restricted Eating on Fat Loss & Health

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: I'm Andrew Huberman, and I'm a professor of neurobiology and ophthalmology at Stanford School of Medicine. Today we're going to talk about how intermittent fasting, aka time-restricted feeding, impacts weight loss, fat loss in particular, muscle maintenance and loss and gain, organ health, such as gut health and liver health, the genome, the epigenome, inflammation, sickness, recovery, and healing from sickness, exercise, cognition, mood, and lifespan.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: I'm going to give you an example of a study that was published a few years ago, 2018, by a colleague of mine at Stanford, Chris Gardner. This paper looked at weight loss in people following one particular diet versus another particular diet over 12 months. The basic conclusion of the study was that there was no significant difference in weight change between people following a healthy, low-fat diet versus a healthy, low-carbohydrate diet with significantly more dietary fats in them. If one's main goal is simply to lose weight, then it really does not matter what one eats, provided that the number of calories burned is higher than the number of calories ingested.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: There is a perfect diet for you. And today I'm going to arm you with the mechanisms and understanding that will allow you to define what that perfect diet is and will allow you to eat on a schedule and to eat the things that are going to best serve your goals. When you eat, typically your blood glucose, your blood sugar will go up. Also insulin levels will go up. How much your glucose and insulin go up depends on what you eat and how much you eat. Simple sugars will raise your insulin and blood glucose more than complex carbohydrates. And grains and breads and pastas will raise your blood glucose more than fibrous carbohydrates like lettuce and broccoli.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: The most important thing to understand is that like everything in biology, this is a process that takes time. Insulin and glucose go up when we eat and it takes some period of time for them to go down. The health benefits from time-restricted feeding occur because certain conditions are met in the brain and body for a certain amount of time. This is the simplest and best way to think about any eating schedule or any eating plan.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: There was a paper in mice titled Time-Restricted Feeding Without Reducing Caloric Intake Prevents Metabolic Diseases in Mice Fed a High Fat Diet. Mice that ate a highly palatable, high fat diet, but only during a restricted feeding window of each 24 hour cycle maintained or lost weight over time. Whereas mice that ingested the same diet, same amount of calories, but had access to those calories around the clock, gained weight, became obese and quite sick. The mice that restricted their feeding window to a particular portion of eight hours of every 24 hour cycle actually showed some improvement in important health markers.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: You may be surprised to learn that 80% of the genes in your body and brain are on a 24 hour schedule. When those genes are high at the appropriate times and low at the appropriate times, your health benefits. When those genes are not expressed at the right times, that's when you get negative health effects. And while this was in mice, we now know that this also occurs in humans. If you want to be healthy, one of the most important things you can do is to eat at the appropriate time of each 24 hour day.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: By eating around the clock, you're making yourself sicker. By eating at restricted periods of time each 24 hour day, you're actually making yourself healthier. Restricting your feeding to a particular window every 24 hour cycle has clearly been shown now in mice and in humans to enhance liver health. The time restricted feeding essentially reversed fatty deposits in the liver or led in many cases to even healthier liver condition.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: It pays off in the metabolic sense and in the health sense and in the weight maintenance or loss sense to not ingest any food in the first hour after waking and potentially for longer. For the two and ideally three hours prior to bedtime, you also don't ingest any food or liquid calories. Starting to eat each day somewhere around 10 a.m. or around noon, and then allowing a feeding window that goes until six or maybe 8 p.m., that seems to me like the kind of schedule that will allow you to get the most out of intermittent fasting.

paragraphs.speaker: andrew
paragraphs.name: Andrew Huberman
paragraphs.text: The seven to nine hour feeding window produces all of the major health benefits of time-restricted feeding, as well as being
